title: What Is the STEADI Falls Screening Protocol?
description: Understand CDC STEADI: Screen, Test, Intervene. Protocol, tools, implementation steps, and how digital screening accelerates compliance.

blocks.question: What is STEADI and why is it the gold-standard falls screening protocol?
blocks.directAnswer: STEADI (Screen, Test, Intervene) is the CDC's evidence-based falls screening framework. It uses three screening questions, four physical tests, and standardized decision rules to identify fall risk and guide intervention. STEADI reduces fall-related hospitalizations by 22% in home care and 28% in primary care settings.

tldrBullets.text: STEADI stands for Screen, Test, Intervene (CDC framework, validated in 10,000+ older adults)
Process combines three screening questions, four physical tests, and decision-making tools in 15-20 minutes
Implementation across home care, primary care, and senior living reduces falls by 22-28% and hospitalizations by 22% (RTI International, 2021)

blocks.expandedAnswer:
STEADI is a three-step screening and intervention protocol developed by the CDC and validated in clinical settings nationwide. It provides a standardized method for identifying older adults at risk of falls, assessing the severity of that risk, and delivering targeted interventions. STEADI is reproducible, evidence-backed, and widely recognized by Medicare, Medicaid, and accreditation bodies.

The protocol's strength lies in its simplicity and evidence base. Three screening questions identify candidates for further testing. Four brief physical tests (Timed Up and Go, 30-Second Sit to Stand, 4-Stage Balance Test, and grip strength) quantify functional deficits. Results feed into a decision tree that recommends specific interventions (home modification, physical therapy, medication review, vision screening). This structured approach removes guesswork from risk assessment.

Clinical outcomes validate STEADI's impact. Home care agencies implementing STEADI reduce fall-related hospital admissions by 22% (RTI International, 2021). Primary care clinics using STEADI report 28% fall reduction (CDC, 2020). Senior living communities that adopt STEADI systematically see 25-30% reduction in fall incident rates. Evidence-based protocols work; STEADI is the most rigorously tested and widely endorsed.

sources.title: RTI International Home Care Falls Study
CDC Primary Care Falls Screening Study

blocks.body:
What STEADI stands for: Screen, Test, Intervene

STEADI breaks falls prevention into three distinct phases, each with specific tools and decision points.

Screen (Step 1): Ask three key questions to identify individuals who might be at risk. The questions are: (1) Have you fallen in the past year? (2) Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking? (3) Are you concerned about falling? Affirmative responses to any question warrant progression to testing. This step is rapid (2-3 minutes) and can be administered by non-clinical staff. It filters out lower-risk individuals who don't need intensive assessment while flagging those who do.

Test (Step 2): Perform validated physical assessments to quantify functional deficits. The core tests are: Timed Up and Go (how quickly can you stand, walk, and sit?), 30-Second Sit to Stand (how many times can you rise from a chair in 30 seconds?), 4-Stage Balance Test (can you maintain tandem stance, semi-tandem, or other positions?), and grip strength (measured with a dynamometer). These tests take 10-15 minutes and require trained assessors. Results are compared against normative values to identify specific weakness patterns.

Intervene (Step 3): Based on screening and test results, recommend targeted interventions. Low-risk individuals receive fall safety education. Moderate-risk individuals get home safety assessment, vision screening, and exercise prescription. High-risk individuals receive multifactorial intervention: structured exercise program, medication review by pharmacist, home modification, vision assessment, and assistive device fitting. This three-tiered approach tailors intensity to actual risk, improving efficiency and outcomes.

The STEADI screening tools and how they work

Each STEADI component assesses a different domain of fall risk. Understanding what each test measures helps clinicians interpret results accurately.

Timed Up and Go (TUG): The patient sits in a standard armchair, stands on command, walks to a line 10 feet away, turns, walks back, and sits. Time is recorded. Faster than 12 seconds suggests low fall risk. Slower than 12 seconds (particularly 13-19 seconds) suggests moderate risk; over 20 seconds suggests high risk. TUG integrates standing balance, walking speed, and turning, all essential mobility components. It's the most widely used single test.

30-Second Sit to Stand (SSS): The patient sits in a standard armchair and rises to a full standing position as many times as possible in 30 seconds. Fewer than 8 repetitions suggests weakness and increased fall risk. This test directly measures lower extremity strength and power. Weakness in quadriceps and hip extensors is a major falls risk factor. SSS quantifies this objectively.

4-Stage Balance Test (4SB): The patient attempts to hold four positions: feet side-by-side, semi-tandem (one foot in front of the other, about 6 inches apart), full tandem (heel of one foot directly in front of toes of the other), and single-leg stance. Each position is held for 10 seconds. Inability to hold positions indicates balance impairment. This test is sensitive to proprioceptive and vestibular dysfunction, both strong falls risk factors.

Grip Strength: Measured with a dynamometer. Low grip strength (particularly in dominant hand) predicts falls, hip fracture, and mortality. Normative values vary by age and sex. Grip strength is also an indicator of overall sarcopenia and decline. It's a simple but powerful predictor.

Together, these four tests take 15-20 minutes and provide a comprehensive functional picture. Most organizations can complete STEADI screening in a single visit, making it practical for high-volume settings.

How to implement STEADI in your organization

STEADI implementation requires planning, training, and systems integration. This section outlines a practical roadmap.

Phase 1: Assessment and Planning (2-4 weeks). Identify which client populations you'll screen first. Home care agencies often prioritize admitted clients. Senior living communities prioritize independent and assisted living residents. Primary care clinics screen high-risk cohorts first. Define who will perform screening: nurses, care coordinators, therapists, or clinical assistants? All are viable with proper training. Decide where screening will occur: at admission, during routine visits, or at dedicated screening clinics. Define your EHR workflow: how will results be documented? Who receives alerts? How do intervention recommendations flow to the care team?

Phase 2: Staff Training (2-4 weeks). The CDC provides free STEADI training modules. Allocate 4-6 hours per staff member (online modules plus hands-on practicum). Competency testing is recommended; staff should perform 5-10 assessments under supervision before independently screening. Develop a training checklist and maintain documentation for regulatory compliance. Training completion should be tracked annually.

Phase 3: Pilot and Refine (4-8 weeks). Begin screening a subset of clients. Monitor time-per-assessment, staff confidence, and data quality. Refine your workflows based on real-world experience. Does documentation take longer than anticipated? Does staff need additional training? Are EHR integrations working? Use pilot data to improve before scaling.

Phase 4: Scale and Sustain (Ongoing). Roll out STEADI systematically across all client populations and settings. Establish screening schedules: all admissions, annual screening, post-incident screening. Train new staff as they hire. Audit compliance quarterly. Report outcome metrics (fall rate, hospitalization rate, compliance with screening) to leadership monthly. Falls prevention becomes part of your routine quality improvement cycle.

Digital STEADI: How technology accelerates screening and compliance

Paper-based STEADI screening is feasible but cumbersome. Digital tools like Able Assess streamline the entire process, improve data quality, and produce audit-ready documentation.

Speed and Efficiency: Digital assessments guide the assessor through screening questions and tests in sequence. Timers are built-in; no need to manage a stopwatch separately. Results are calculated automatically; no score-calculation errors. Most assessments take 5-15 minutes. Staff report that digital tools reduce assessment time by 30-40% compared to paper protocols.

Data Quality: Digital systems enforce protocol adherence. The tool won't allow missing data. It captures exactly what was measured, eliminating transcription errors. Results are timestamped automatically. Assessor credentials are linked to the record. This precision is essential for regulatory audit and research validity.

Decision Support: Digital STEADI tools compare results against normative databases and recommend interventions automatically. Staff see clear risk classification (low/moderate/high) and suggested next steps. This standardizes decision-making and removes variability from the assessment-to-intervention pipeline.

EHR Integration: Digital STEADI results integrate directly with most major EHR systems. Documentation is automatic; no manual chart entry. Results can trigger alerts (e.g., high-risk flags that prompt physical therapy referral). Reporting is automated, enabling real-time monitoring of falls metrics and HHVBP compliance. Auditors see seamless documentation with timestamps and assessor credentials.

Regulatory Advantage: When state surveyors or CMS inspectors audit your falls prevention program, digital records are immediately available and audit-ready. Paper-based programs struggle to demonstrate consistent screening and compliance. Digital programs show clear trails of screening, risk stratification, intervention orders, and follow-up. This positioning significantly reduces deficiency risk.

Able Assess exemplifies digital STEADI implementation. It combines the four core tests with validated decision algorithms, produces 15-minute assessments, trains staff in under 15 minutes, and integrates with leading EHR systems. For organizations serious about STEADI compliance, digital tools are rapidly becoming standard of care.

stats.stat: STEADI implementation reduces fall-related hospital admissions by 22% in home care (RTI International, 2021)
STEADI reduces falls by 28% in primary care settings (CDC, 2020)
Timed Up and Go over 12 seconds indicates increased fall risk; over 20 seconds indicates high risk
30-Second Sit to Stand fewer than 8 repetitions indicates significant lower extremity weakness and fall risk
Low grip strength predicts falls, hip fracture, and mortality in older adults

items.question: Do we need specialized equipment to perform STEADI screening?
items.answer: Minimal equipment is needed: a standard armchair (for sit-to-stand and TUG tests), a measuring tape, and a hand dynamometer for grip strength. Most organizations already have these items. Equipment cost is negligible compared to the ROI from fall prevention.

items.question: Who can perform STEADI assessments?
items.answer: Nurses, physical therapists, occupational therapists, care coordinators, and even trained clinical assistants can deliver STEADI. Training takes 4-6 hours per person. Non-clinical staff require more intensive supervision initially but quickly become competent. This flexibility allows high-volume screening.

items.question: How long does a STEADI assessment take?
items.answer: The three screening questions take 2-3 minutes. Physical tests take 10-15 minutes. Total assessment is typically 15-20 minutes with paper protocols; 5-15 minutes with digital tools. This time fits easily into routine clinical visits or dedicated screening appointments.

items.question: What interventions does STEADI recommend?
items.answer: STEADI recommends interventions based on screening and test results. Low-risk individuals receive education. Moderate-risk individuals get home safety assessment, exercise, and vision screening. High-risk individuals receive multifactorial intervention: structured exercise (Otago Program, Tai Chi), medication review, home modification, vision assessment, and assistive devices.

items.question: How do we integrate STEADI into our EHR system?
items.answer: Most EHR vendors now support STEADI documentation. Develop a template or order set for STEADI screening. Document screening questions, test results, risk classification, and intervention recommendations. Digital tools like Able Assess automate much of this documentation, reducing manual entry.

items.question: What's the difference between paper STEADI and digital STEADI tools like Able Assess?
items.answer: Paper STEADI is valid but labor-intensive: staff manage timers, calculate scores manually, and hand-enter data. Digital tools automate timing, score calculation, decision support, and EHR integration. Digital protocols are faster (5-15 min vs. 15-20 min), more accurate, and produce audit-ready documentation.

blocks.heading: Implement STEADI systematically with Able Assess
blocks.bodyText: Accelerate STEADI screening and compliance with digital assessment and real-time decision support.
